content.text: Raw text from the real world is messy: HTML tags, non-breaking spaces, mixed Unicode forms, PII buried in logs, duplicate paragraphs, and boilerplate footers that drown meaningful content.
Text preprocessing

is the set of cleaning and normalisation operations applied before text reaches a tokeniser, embedding model, or vector index. Getting it right improves retrieval precision, reduces token cost, and keeps sensitive data out of places it should not be.
What it is

Text preprocessing is a pipeline of deterministic transformations that convert raw bytes or strings into a consistent, clean form suitable for downstream NLP tasks. Unlike a machine-learning model that learns to handle noise, a preprocessing pipeline is hand-crafted: every rule is explicit and reproducible. Classic NLP required heavy preprocessing to feed bag-of-words or TF-IDF models. Modern large language models handle more variation natively through subword tokenisation, but preprocessing remains essential at the
document
level — before text ever reaches the tokeniser.

The typical operations are: Unicode normalisation, byte-order-mark removal, markup stripping (HTML, Markdown, LaTeX), whitespace collapse, optional lowercasing, sentence segmentation, PII redaction, language detection, deduplication, and length filtering. In agentic pipelines the most important subset is usually Unicode normalisation + markup stripping + whitespace cleanup + PII redaction, because these directly affect retrieval quality and compliance.
Why it matters

Agents ingest text from heterogeneous sources: web-scraped HTML, PDF conversions, Slack threads, Jira tickets, Confluence exports, customer emails, database dumps. Each source has its own encoding quirks, markup dialect, and noise patterns. Without a shared hygiene layer these quirks propagate into embeddings and indexes, causing silent failures.
Three failure modes are most common in production. First,
retrieval misses

: the string "café" composed as U+0063 U+0061 U+0066 U+00E9 and as U+0063 U+0061 U+0066 U+0065 U+0301 (e + combining accent) produce different embedding vectors if normalisation is skipped, so the same word indexed twice never deduplicates. Second,
token bloat
: an HTML page with navigation, headers, and cookie banners can triple the token count of its meaningful text body; every LLM call pays for those tokens. Third,
privacy leaks
: without PII redaction, customer emails, SSNs, and API keys flow into vector databases and LLM context windows, creating compliance risk.
Benefits
Consistent retrieval
— Unicode normalisation collapses variant forms so the same semantic token always maps to one index entry, improving recall.
Token efficiency
— stripping HTML boilerplate and repeated navigation text cuts average chunk size by 30–50 %, reducing LLM API costs.
Privacy compliance
— PII redaction prevents personal data from reaching external APIs or persisting in logs.
Deterministic behaviour
— rule-based transforms are reproducible; the same input always produces the same output, unlike model-based alternatives.
Faster deduplication
— once text is normalised to a canonical form, exact-hash deduplication reliably eliminates near-identical documents.
Eval parity
— identical preprocessing on training, indexing, and inference data prevents train/serve skew from distorting benchmark scores.
Trade-offs
Over-normalisation destroys signal
— aggressive lowercasing breaks case-sensitive identifiers (API keys, enum names, Python class names); stemming corrupts code snippets.
Markup stripping loses structure
— HTML tables, ordered lists, and headings contain semantic structure that some tasks (table QA, document classification) need. Stripping them loses hierarchy.
Language-specific rules fail on multilingual traffic
— stopword lists, sentence tokenisers, and stemming algorithms are language-scoped. Applying English rules to French text silently garbles it.
Latency tax
— each preprocessing step adds CPU time. For real-time chat agents every millisecond matters; a heavy spaCy pipeline adding 50–100 ms per message is a measurable regression.
Maintenance burden
— regex patterns for PII, URL cleaning rules, and whitespace heuristics must be maintained and versioned separately from model weights.
False-positive redaction
— overly broad PII patterns can redact code patterns or ISBNs that happen to look like SSNs, corrupting content.
How it works
Unicode normalisation
Unicode defines four normalisation forms. For NLP the most useful is
NFKC

(Compatibility Decomposition followed by Canonical Composition). NFKC collapses compatibility variants — fullwidth Latin letters, fractions like ¼, ligatures like ﬁ, and superscripts — into their base equivalents, and composes decomposed characters (e + combining accent → é) into precomposed forms. The Python standard library handles this in one line.
NFD is useful when you need to
strip diacritics

: decompose first, then filter out Unicode category Mn (non-spacing marks). NFC is the default on most web APIs and is safe for round-tripping. NFKD decomposes without recomposing; useful for building ASCII-folding search. For RAG pipelines NFKC is the default choice.
Markup stripping

HTML stripping should use a proper parser, not a regex. Regexes fail on nested tags, CDATA sections, and HTML entities. BeautifulSoup with the lxml or html.parser backend handles malformed markup gracefully. The separator argument controls what replaces removed tags — a space prevents words from concatenating across tag boundaries.

Markdown stripping is trickier: backtick code blocks, ATX headings, and link syntax must be handled without collapsing multi-line blocks. The markdownify or mistune libraries can convert Markdown to plain text, or you can render to HTML first, then strip HTML.
Whitespace normalisation

After markup removal, text often contains runs of spaces, tabs, non-breaking spaces (U+00A0), zero-width spaces (U+200B), and paragraph-separator characters. A single regex replacement collapses all Unicode whitespace (\s+ in Python matches all Unicode whitespace when the UNICODE flag is set, which is the default for str patterns in Python 3). Trimming leading and trailing whitespace is separate.
PII redaction

Regex-based redaction is fast and auditable but limited to known patterns (email addresses, phone numbers, SSNs, credit card numbers). For more comprehensive PII detection — names, addresses, passport numbers — a trained NER model (spaCy, Presidio, AWS Comprehend) is necessary. The trade-off is latency vs. coverage. Many production systems combine both: fast regex for structured patterns, NER for entities.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a preprocessing pipeline feeding a RAG vector store. Text flows left to right through cleaning stages, then continues in the second row through language detection, sentence splitting, and deduplication, before being chunked, embedded, and stored. Each stage is independently swappable — for example, the PII redaction step can be rule-based or model-based without affecting other stages.

attrs.alt: Text preprocessing pipeline for RAG — Raw Text → Unicode NFKC → Markup Strip → Whitespace Norm → PII Redact → Lang Detect → Sentence Split → Dedup → Clean Text → Chunker → Embedding → Vector DB

content.text: The pipeline has two key properties. First, it is
stateless

— each document is processed independently, so stages parallelise trivially. Second, the clean-text artefact is stored alongside (not instead of) the original, so the original can be displayed to users while the clean form drives retrieval. This dual-storage pattern is important for auditability.
When to use it

Apply preprocessing whenever text crosses a trust or format boundary: when ingesting documents from external sources, before inserting into a vector index, before evaluation harness scoring, and before sending user content to a third-party LLM API.
Use it when:
Indexing heterogeneous document sources (web, PDF, email, Markdown).
Building a RAG pipeline where retrieval quality depends on canonical text forms.
Logging user conversations that may contain PII.
Running evaluations across datasets produced by different tools.
Deduplicating large corpora before fine-tuning or embedding.
Skip or minimise preprocessing when:
Feeding text directly to a chat LLM where structural signals (tone, capitalisation) carry meaning.
The source is already clean, controlled-format text (generated JSON, programmatically built prompts).
Latency is critical and the source is known to be a clean format.
Code files are the input — almost all preprocessing steps are harmful to source code.
How to implement

A minimal but production-grade preprocessing function for a RAG ingestion pipeline has five stages: decode, normalise, strip, redact, and clean. Each is a pure function — no side effects, no global state — so the pipeline is easy to test and reason about.

import unicodedata import re from bs4 import BeautifulSoup # ── Stage 1: Unicode normalisation ───────────────────────────────── def normalise_unicode(text: str) -> str: """Collapse compatibility variants and compose combining characters.""" return unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", text) # ── Stage 2: HTML / markup stripping ─────────────────────────────── def strip_html(text: str) -> str: """Remove HTML tags; use a real parser, not regex.""" if "<" not in text: return text return BeautifulSoup(text, "html.parser").get_text(separator=" ") # ── Stage 3: Whitespace normalisation ────────────────────────────── # \s in Python 3 str patterns matches ALL Unicode whitespace including # \u00a0 (non-breaking space), \u200b (zero-width space), \u2028 (line sep). _WS = re.compile(r"\s+") def normalise_whitespace(text: str) -> str: return _WS.sub(" ", text).strip() # ── Stage 4: PII redaction ───────────────────────────────────────── _PII = [ (re.compile(r"\b[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+\b"), "[EMAIL]"), (re.compile(r"\b(?:\d[ -]?){13,16}\b"), "[CARD]"), (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b"), "[SSN]"), (re.compile(r"\b(?:\+?1[\s.-]?)?\(?\d{3}\)?[\s.-]?\d{3}[\s.-]?\d{4}\b"), "[PHONE]"), ] def redact_pii(text: str) -> str: for pattern, placeholder in _PII: text = pattern.sub(placeholder, text) return text # ── Composed pipeline ────────────────────────────────────────────── def preprocess(raw: str, redact: bool = True) -> str: text = normalise_unicode(raw) text = strip_html(text) text = normalise_whitespace(text) if redact: text = redact_pii(text) return text # ── Quick test ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── if __name__ == "__main__": html = """
Skip to content

Invoice for John Smith

Contact: john.smith@acme.com   Phone: 555-867-5309

SSN: 123-45-6789 — Card: 4111 1111 1111 1111

""" result = preprocess(html) print(result) # → Skip to content Invoice for John Smith Contact: [EMAIL] Phone: [PHONE] # SSN: [SSN] — Card: [CARD]
Install dependencies:
pip install beautifulsoup4 lxml
. The
lxml
parser is faster than the built-in
html.parser
; swap the backend string to switch. For production, pin the version of BeautifulSoup to avoid behaviour changes across releases.
Examples
Example 1 — Unicode deduplication failure without NFKC
This example demonstrates why two strings that look identical in a terminal can fail equality without normalisation, and how NFKC fixes it.

import unicodedata

# "café" — two visually identical strings, different byte sequences
composed   = "caf\u00e9"          # precomposed é  (NFC form)
decomposed = "cafe\u0301"         # e + combining acute accent (NFD form)

print(composed == decomposed)                      # False — different bytes
print(len(composed), len(decomposed))              # 4   5

# After NFKC normalisation both map to the same string
a = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", composed)
b = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", decomposed)
print(a == b)                                      # True
print(a.encode("utf-8"))                           # b'caf\xc3\xa9'

# Practical impact: vector index deduplication
index = {}
def index_doc(text, doc_id):
    key = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", text)
    index[key] = doc_id

index_doc(composed,   "doc-1")
index_doc(decomposed, "doc-2")   # overwrites doc-1 — correct dedup
print(len(index))                # 1, not 2
Example 2 — Full RAG ingestion pipeline with chunking
A realistic ingestion routine that preprocesses a raw HTML document and splits it into overlapping chunks ready for embedding.

import unicodedata, re from bs4 import BeautifulSoup from typing import Iterator def preprocess(raw: str, redact_pii: bool = True) -> str: text = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", raw) if "<" in text: text = BeautifulSoup(text, "html.parser").get_text(separator=" ") text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip() if redact_pii: text = re.sub(r"\b[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+\b", "[EMAIL]", text) return text def chunk_text( text: str, chunk_size: int = 400, # characters (approx 100 tokens) overlap: int = 80, ) -> Iterator[str]: """Yield overlapping character-window chunks.""" start = 0 while start < len(text): end = start + chunk_size yield text[start:end] if end >= len(text): break start += chunk_size - overlap def ingest_document(html: str, doc_id: str) -> list[dict]: clean = preprocess(html) chunks = list(chunk_text(clean)) return [ {"doc_id": doc_id, "chunk_index": i, "text": chunk} for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks) ] # Demo sample_html = """ Company Policy
Home | About | Contact

Remote Work Policy

Employees must log hours in Jira. Contact hr@company.com for access.

All equipment requests go to it-helpdesk@company.com with your manager's approval. Equipment is shipped within 5 business days.

© 2024 Company Inc. All rights reserved.
""" chunks = ingest_document(sample_html, "policy-001") for c in chunks: print(f"[{c['chunk_index']}] {c['text'][:80]}...") # [0] Home | About | Contact Remote Work Policy Employees must log hours in J... # [1] Jira. Contact [EMAIL] for access. All equipment requests go to [EMAIL] wi...
Real-world example

A legal document Q&A agent ingests 50,000 Confluence wiki pages as its knowledge base. The raw exports are HTML with navigation menus, breadcrumb trails, comment threads, and attachment previews embedded in every page. Without preprocessing, the average page contributes 1,200 tokens, of which only ~35 % is the actual article body. With preprocessing — HTML stripping, boilerplate removal using known CSS selectors, Unicode normalisation, and deduplication via MinHash — the average drops to 420 tokens (65 % reduction) with a negligible drop in recall on the QA benchmark.

The production pipeline uses the following structure: a FastAPI ingestion service receives webhook events from Confluence on page update, spawns an async preprocessing task that applies the five-stage pipeline, writes the clean text to PostgreSQL, and enqueues a chunking + embedding job. The original HTML is preserved in object storage for display. This dual-storage pattern means engineers can re-index with a new embedding model without re-fetching source documents.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Different preprocessing at index time vs. query time

If documents are lowercased before embedding but queries are not, or if HTML is stripped at index time but queries come in with Markdown syntax intact, retrieval scores are systematically degraded. The fix is a single shared
preprocess()
function imported by both the ingestion service and the query handler.
Mistake 2 — Stripping markup from code-heavy documents

A README.md file contains fenced code blocks. Stripping all Markdown removes the language identifiers and the code-block delimiters, destroying the distinction between prose and code. For code repositories, preserve code blocks by extracting them to separate chunks with a
type:code
metadata tag, and strip Markdown only from prose paragraphs.
Mistake 3 — Applying NFKC to binary or base64 content

NFKC replaces superscript digits (⁰¹²³) with their ASCII equivalents. If a document contains base64-encoded payloads or chemical formulas using superscript notation, NFKC silently modifies them. Apply normalisation only after confirmed-clean prose extraction.
Best practices
Always apply NFKC before any string comparison or hashing
— this is the single highest-value preprocessing step for multilingual content.
Store original alongside clean
— never discard the source; re-indexing with a new model requires only re-running the pipeline on stored originals.
Version preprocessing rules alongside embedding models
— a preprocessing change invalidates the index. Tag the index with the preprocessing version.
Unit test edge cases
— write tests for RTL text, emoji, zero-width joiners, HTML entities, and nested tags before deploying.
Benchmark the PII redaction recall
— test your regex suite against a labelled PII corpus regularly; new patterns (IBAN, NHS numbers) need adding.
Use a shared module
— import one preprocessing module in all services rather than copy-pasting logic, so changes propagate atomically.
Log the clean text, not the raw text
— once PII is redacted, ensure log pipelines never bypass preprocessing.
Performance & scalability

The CPU cost of a typical preprocessing pipeline scales linearly with text length. On a modern server core, NFKC normalisation of 10,000 characters takes under 1 ms. HTML parsing with BeautifulSoup (html.parser) runs at roughly 5–10 MB/s; switching to lxml raises throughput to 40–80 MB/s. Regex-based PII redaction at ~1 MB/s is often the bottleneck when the pattern list grows large.

For high-throughput ingestion (millions of documents per day) preprocessing is typically parallelised using process pools rather than threads, because the GIL prevents true parallelism for CPU-bound Python code. A typical architecture uses a message queue (Kafka, SQS) and a pool of preprocessing workers, each running a single-threaded
preprocess()
function. With 8 cores at 80 MB/s each, the pipeline can process roughly 55 GB/hour.
For deduplication at scale, exact hashing (SHA-256 of the NFKC-normalised text) handles perfect duplicates with negligible overhead. Near-duplicate detection uses MinHash LSH (the
datasketch
library), which approximates Jaccard similarity in O(n) space using a configurable number of hash permutations. At 128 permutations the false positive rate for 80 % Jaccard threshold is under 1 %.
Tooling & ecosystem
Python standard library
The
unicodedata
module provides NFKC/NFC/NFD/NFKD normalisation and character category lookup at C speed — no install required. The
re
module handles the common whitespace and PII patterns.
BeautifulSoup + lxml

BeautifulSoup 4 with the lxml parser is the standard HTML/XML stripping solution. It handles malformed HTML, encoding detection via the charset meta tag, and CSS selector-based boilerplate removal. Install:
pip install beautifulsoup4 lxml
spaCy
spaCy provides sentence segmentation, tokenisation, language detection, and NER-based PII detection in one package. The
en_core_web_sm
model adds NER for PERSON, ORG, GPE, MONEY, and DATE. Install:
pip install spacy && python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm

import spacy

nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")

def sentence_split(text: str) -> list[str]:
    doc = nlp(text)
    return [sent.text.strip() for sent in doc.sents]

def redact_with_ner(text: str) -> str:
    doc = nlp(text)
    result = text
    # Replace in reverse order to preserve character offsets
    for ent in sorted(doc.ents, key=lambda e: e.start_char, reverse=True):
        if ent.label_ in {"PERSON", "ORG", "GPE"}:
            result = result[:ent.start_char] + f"[{ent.label_}]" + result[ent.end_char:]
    return result

text = "Alice Smith at Acme Corp sent a report from New York."
print(redact_with_ner(text))
# [PERSON] at [ORG] sent a report from [GPE].
Microsoft Presidio

Presidio is an open-source PII detection and anonymisation library that combines regex, NER, and context rules. It supports 50+ entity types across multiple languages and integrates with spaCy models. Install:
pip install presidio-analyzer presidio-anonymizer
. Presidio is the right choice when compliance requirements demand auditable, configurable PII handling rather than hand-written regexes.
langdetect / fastText
Language detection uses either the
langdetect

library (port of Google's langdetect, supports 55 languages) or the fastText language identification model (supports 176 languages with sub-millisecond inference). For agents handling international user input, language detection enables routing to the correct sentence tokeniser and NER model.

For most RAG pipelines the right stack is: regex pipeline + BeautifulSoup for the ingestion path, with Presidio added where regulatory compliance demands it. LLM-based preprocessing is only warranted for small volumes where a human-equivalent understanding of context is required to correctly anonymise content.
Security & reliability
PII leakage

The most critical reliability requirement is that PII redaction must happen before text reaches any external service: the vector database, the LLM API, and the logging infrastructure. The correct architecture enforces this by making the preprocessing step a mandatory gateway — all ingestion code imports and calls the same
preprocess()
function and has no path that bypasses it.
ReDoS risk in regex patterns

Pathological regex patterns applied to attacker-controlled text can cause catastrophic backtracking (Regular Expression Denial of Service, ReDoS). PII patterns for email addresses and URLs are particularly prone to this. Mitigation: use the
regex
library instead of
re
(it provides possessive quantifiers and atomic groups that prevent backtracking), set a timeout on all regex operations using
signal.alarm
, and enforce a document size limit before preprocessing.
Encoding injection

A malicious document can embed Unicode direction-override characters (U+202E RLO, U+200F RLM) to make displayed text appear different from its byte content. NFKC does not strip these characters. For security-critical pipelines, explicitly remove Unicode bidi-control characters (category Cf) after NFKC normalisation.
Reliability of HTML parsers

BeautifulSoup with lxml will parse almost any malformed HTML without raising exceptions. However, extremely large documents (> 50 MB) can cause the parser to allocate proportional memory. Set a byte limit on input size before parsing, and run parsing in a subprocess with a memory limit for untrusted inputs.
Mental model

Think of preprocessing as the kitchen prep before cooking. The chef (the LLM or embedding model) is skilled and can work with imperfect ingredients — but grit in the lettuce, mud on the carrots, and bones left in the fish still ruin the dish. Preprocessing is washing, peeling, and deboning: invisible when done right, catastrophic when skipped. The more heterogeneous and untrusted the ingredients (user-uploaded documents, scraped web pages), the more rigorous the prep needs to be.
Key takeaways
NFKC normalisation is the highest-value single step
— it collapses Unicode variant forms so the same semantic content always hashes and embeds identically.
Use a real HTML parser, not regex
— BeautifulSoup with lxml handles malformed HTML reliably and is fast enough for most batch pipelines.
PII redaction must precede every external service call
— enforce this structurally with a shared module, not by convention.
Keep preprocessing identical on index and query paths
— mismatched normalisation is a silent retrieval bug that is hard to diagnose.
Store the original; index the clean copy
— this enables re-indexing without re-fetching source documents.
Version preprocessing rules alongside embedding models
— a preprocessing change invalidates the vector index and must trigger a full re-index.
Benchmark, don't assume
— measure token reduction, retrieval recall improvement, and PII redaction coverage on your actual corpus before optimising.
Parallelise with process pools
— the GIL prevents threading from accelerating CPU-bound preprocessing; multiprocessing scales linearly with cores.
